props.covers: The data model at the core of the interactive canvas engine: the document schema, the typed action vocabulary, the single reducer that mutates it, and the pure geometry helpers it relies on.

text.insert: The model layer is the data core of

: a FigJam-parity, typed-action, world-space interactive canvas engine, extracted into its own git submodule. It defines what a canvas document is (schema), the closed vocabulary of ways it can change (actions), the single function that performs every change (the reducer), and a small set of pure geometry helpers those changes lean on. Everything above this layer — pointer interaction, connector routing, rendering, the chrome UI, the editor shell — reads and writes through these types and functions rather than inventing its own notion of canvas state.
, is the serializable, versioned shape of a canvas:

world-space polyline override that wins over auto-routing when present.
cross-reference target to an object, with a resolution
attach a note or an agent-request to an object, connection, or arbitrary region, with an
— a nesting/grouping box; other objects reference it via
— a note card; can carry an
— a small pinned marker glyph, distinct from the

— added in D16, expanded vocabulary for reference diagrams.
— added in D16, expanded vocabulary for reference diagrams.

That's 37 object types in total. The D16 wave (document, person, database, chat) expanded the vocabulary for reference-style architecture diagrams; the W2 wave (section, pill, arrow-shape, predefined-process, code-block, chip-icon) added FigJam-parity sections plus the V2 Flow shape set; the W5 wave (ellipse, triangle, parallelogram, pentagon, octagon, star, plus, chevron, folder, document-stack, off-page-connector, trapezoid, manual-input, hexagon, internal-storage, or-junction, summing-junction, cylinder-horizontal, page-corner, icon) closed out FigJam's Basic/Flowchart/Advanced picker tiers — 19 native shape primitives plus one icon-glyph family (
field drawn from a 26-value CanvasIconGlyph enum) covering the Advanced tier's 26 component icons in one type instead of 26 bespoke ones.
Color resolution: tone, palette token, section tint
is the base semantic bucket (neutral, input, process, decision, memory, agent, warning, annotation) that most object types resolve their default fill/stroke from.
(D16) is a coarser color-as-meaning preset — process=blue, input=green, hot=orange/red, memory=purple, note=yellow — and wins over
(W4) wins over both, letting the FigJam pastel-pair palette apply directly.
objects sit outside this ladder entirely: their fill/chip color comes from a separate
family (green, purple, orange, yellow, gray, white, pink, red, blue, teal) resolved against FigJam's own section color tokens, not the tone/paletteToken system.
is a hand-written validator over an
input — no schema library, just explicit type-guard functions per field. It returns a discriminated
. The key distinction is soft vs. hard: an unknown
is just dropped with a warning and normalization continues, while a missing geometry, a duplicate id, an unknown object type, or a section missing its
is a hard error that fails the whole document.
is the one place that turns a failed validation into a thrown error, for callers that want fail-fast semantics instead of handling the result union themselves.
, is a single discriminated union with exactly 22 variants, every one prefixed
. This closed vocabulary is the entire surface area through which a canvas document can change — nothing mutates a document by any other path. Alongside it,
select, hand, container, process, decision, text, sticky, source-node, annotation-marker, annotation
section, pill, arrow-shape, predefined-process, code-block, chip-icon
— replaces the current selection (none / objects / connection / annotation / region).
— creates one new object of a given type, with default geometry/tone/shape if not supplied, and selects it.
— bulk-inserts objects (and optional connections) in one history entry, remapping ids on collision; used for paste.
— patches one object's fields (geometry is re-snapped, style is shallow-merged).
— bulk geometry patch for many objects at once (drag/section-carry), with an opt-out of history recording for live-preview frames.
— sets one object's width/height, snapped unless opted out.
— nudges every selected object by (dx, dy), snapped unless opted out.
— clones the selected objects (and connections between them) offset by +24/+24, and selects the clones.
— removes the current selection (objects, or the one selected connection) and any connections/links/annotations that referenced it.
— creates a connection between two existing objects, optionally with anchors and a precise relative
— patches a connection's fields, validating that any new endpoints reference real objects and aren't self-loops.
— drag-to-connect gesture: connects to an existing object under the drop point, or creates a new process object at the drop point and connects to it, in one history entry.
— attaches a note or agent-request annotation to an object, connection, or region.
— shape-swaps an object to a different type in place, preserving geometry/label/parentId while re-deriving
and seeding or clearing section-only title/tint fields.
— reparents objects into a container (or clears parent), rejecting cycles and non-container targets.
— aligns selected objects along an edge or center axis, via
— spaces three or more selected objects evenly along an axis.
— resizes a container to the padded bounding box of its children.
— re-derives every link's resolved/stale/missing/unresolved status against a set of known and stale paths.
— pops the most recent document off history.past, pushing the current document onto history.future.
— the inverse of undo, replaying a previously undone document.
— replaces the entire state with a fresh document, clearing history and selection.
Most actions record a history entry via an internal
(source, human-readable summary, and the specific object/connection/annotation ids touched) — this is what backs both the undo ledger and any "what just changed" highlight in a consuming UI.
is the one function that turns
into a new state, and it is the only place a document is ever mutated. Interactive UI gestures — drags, resizes, connector drops, keyboard nudges, all wired up in
values that an external agent or automation script would dispatch to make the identical change programmatically. Neither path has a shortcut around the other: a pointer drag and an agent's

call are, to the reducer, indistinguishable inputs.

This single choke point is what guarantees two properties hold regardless of an action's origin: consistent undo/history (every mutating action goes through the same
bookkeeping, so a human edit and an agent edit undo identically), and connector-waypoint reconciliation (every action that can move or resize an object flows through the same post-action

pass — see below — so no caller can accidentally leave a connection's explicit waypoints stale). Splitting the reducer, or giving any caller a way to patch the document object directly, would let these guarantees diverge quietly between human and agent-driven edits.

props.title: Waypoint reconciliation rides inside the reducer

text.insert: ) with a stale-waypoint choke point: after any action that changed the document (excluding undo/redo/reset, which restore stored documents verbatim),

compares each explicitly-waypointed connection's two endpoint owners before and after. If both owners translated by the same delta with no resize (a rigid move — a multi-select drag, a nudge, a section carrying its members), every waypoint shifts by that same delta, preserving trunk-and-branch fan shapes. If the owners moved asymmetrically or either was resized, the waypoints are dropped so the connector falls back to obstacle-avoiding auto-routing — FigJam's own re-route-on-asymmetric-move behavior. Because this runs inside the single reducer choke point, the waypoint change shares the triggering action's own history entry: one undo restores both geometry and waypoints together.
is a set of pure, DOM-free geometry functions operating purely on plain numbers and
at a 16px grid), anchor-point resolution for connection endpoints (
), bounding-box math over one or many geometries (
), align/distribute layout math, container-fit sizing, and stable id generation (

is the FigJam section-capture algorithm: it computes which objects a section "carries" when dragged purely from geometric overlap (an object is captured once its overlap with the section's bounds reaches a threshold fraction of the object's own area) — never via persisted
membership. Capture is recursive: a captured nested section pulls in its own captured members transitively, so dragging an outer section carries everything nested inside it.
is a type-only vendored copy of
) originally defined in the host Spectre application's own docs-model. It exists here purely so this package has zero runtime dependency back on that host app: the canvas engine only needs the
validator, which stays host-side. The file's own comment is explicit that the two copies must be kept in sync by hand if either evolves — there is no build-time link between them, by design.
There's a fitting irony here: this very doc.json's own
span attributes — the ones wrapping every
shape. The one cross-reference type this package vendors to stay decoupled from its host is the same type structuring the prose that documents it.
— whether triggered by pointer interaction, keyboard shortcuts, the chrome UI, or an external agent — must be expressed as a
. Do not hand-roll ad hoc state mutations elsewhere (e.g. mutating

directly, or writing a parallel update path that bypasses the reducer) — doing so silently breaks undo/redo history and skips the waypoint-reconciliation pass, producing state that looks fine until the next drag or undo exposes the drift.
, following the project-wide convention of keeping tests next to the source they exercise rather than in a separate top-level test tree. Three files cover this layer:

(the largest file, exercising document validation and the full action/reducer vocabulary — object and connection CRUD, selection, undo/redo, alignment/distribution, container fitting, link status resolution),
— normalization during validation and the rigid-translate vs. drop-on-asymmetric-move reconciliation behavior in the reducer), and
(the W2 wave's section capture/tint/title validation and the new V2 Flow shape vocabulary — pill, arrow-shape, predefined-process, code-block, chip-icon).
covers how pointer gestures — drag, resize, connector-drop, section-carry — get translated into the
values this document describes, and how
dispatches them through the same reducer an external agent would use.

props.title: Section membership is geometric, never persisted parentId

text.insert: computes which objects a section "carries" when dragged purely from bounds overlap — an object is captured once its overlap with the section's bounds reaches

(0.6, i.e. 60%) of the OBJECT's own area, recomputed fresh on every drag rather than read from any stored relationship. Capture is recursive: a captured nested section pulls in its own captured members transitively. Do not attempt to read section membership from
— sections never set or rely on it for this purpose.
Z-order: sections always render below every non-section object (
, with the connector SVG layer between them at
), so shapes placed "on" a section visually sit on its surface. Among multiple sections,
computes nesting depth from the same 60%-bounds-containment reading

uses, and paints deeper (more-nested) sections after their ancestors — so a nested section's tint visibly layers on top rather than blending underneath. Ties (non-section-vs-non-section, or sibling sections at the same depth) fall back to the document's own
Added in W2 — a true stadium/pill shape from the V2 Flow vocabulary. Default geometry

— despite the schema.ts comment's phrasing, the tokenizer actually lives under
fixed tokens, not the tone system),
) is split on newlines; each line renders as its own row with a right-aligned line-number gutter (24px wide,
recognizes comments, strings, a fixed keyword set, and capitalized identifiers (colored as a type/class name);
splits quoted-string spans from plain spans, coloring a quoted string followed by
as a key versus a value, and recognizing

as keywords; any other language falls back to untokenized plain text. The tokenizer's own header comment is explicit it is "NOT a real syntax-highlighting engine" — no grammar, no AST. Rendered on a fixed Dracula-theme background (
, 10px corner radius, monospace 13px/1.5) — code-block is the one type that renders body-only: it carries no label chrome at all, even though
is still a required field on the object.

point) is a plain number pair in a single flat world-space coordinate system — logical px, no separate "world" vs. "screen" unit split baked into the schema. The origin
is not pinned to any object; a document's optional
) describes a nominal canvas footprint starting at the origin, but objects are free to have negative
always recomputes the real bounding box from the canvas-base rect plus every object's actual geometry, padded 80px by default, rather than trusting
All geometry is snapped to a
(round-to-nearest-multiple, with width/height floored at one grid cell) — this is a model-layer concern independent of the render layer's own adaptive dot-grid (
= 8, doubling/halving to stay in a comfortable on-screen pitch band as zoom changes; see
for that). The two grids are unrelated in size (16px snap vs. 8px-based adaptive dots) and serve different purposes: one is placement snapping, the other is visual density.
The default sizes in the per-type reference above (set by
) are creation-time seeds only, chosen for a reasonable initial placement — they are not the same numbers as
is 416x420, sampled from real FigJam exports, versus
as the visual-fidelity source of truth (corner radii, stroke widths, font sizes, per-shape color tokens) and
as just the placement convenience every
call falls back to when no explicit
moves a selection's shared bounding-box edge or center axis (left/center-x/right/top/center-y/bottom) to align every selected object along it;
(3+ objects required) spaces objects evenly between the first and last member's position along an axis, by sorting on that axis and interpolating equal gaps;
-type object to the padded (32px default) bounding box of its
-linked children. All three are pure document-in/document-out functions — no mutation, no history bookkeeping of their own — which is why the
actions exist as thin reducer cases that call them and then run the result through
ellipse — added in W5; a true ellipse inscribed in the bounding box.
triangle — added in W5; an isosceles triangle with an up/down direction (defaults to up).
parallelogram — added in W5; a skewed quadrilateral with a left/right direction (defaults to right).
pentagon — added in W5; a 5-point regular polygon.
octagon — added in W5; an 8-point regular polygon.
star — added in W5; a 5-point star polygon.
plus — added in W5; a 12-point cross polygon; renders no visible label text.
chevron — added in W5; a fat-chevron polygon distinct from arrow-shape, with a left/right direction (defaults to right).
folder — added in W5; a manila-folder silhouette with a tab notch.
document-stack — added in W5; offset duplicate document silhouettes suggesting multiple stacked files.
off-page-connector — added in W5; a downward-pointing pentagon.
trapezoid — added in W5; a 4-point trapezoid (manual-operation flowchart symbol).
manual-input — added in W5; a rectangle with a slanted top edge.
hexagon — added in W5; a 6-point regular hexagon (flowchart preparation symbol).
internal-storage — added in W5; a rectangle with interior top-row and left-column rule lines.
or-junction — added in W5; a circle with an inscribed "+"; renders no visible label text.
summing-junction — added in W5; a circle with an inscribed "x"; renders no visible label text.
cylinder-horizontal — added in W5; a horizontal-orientation cylinder, distinct from database's vertical cylinder.
page-corner — added in W5; the dog-eared/folded-corner page silhouette document used before W5.
icon — added in W5; a single icon-glyph family parameterized by a required icon field (26-value CanvasIconGlyph enum), covering FigJam's Advanced picker tier.
Added in W5 — a true ellipse inscribed in the object's bounding box. Default geometry
, default tone "neutral" (inert — ellipse resolves color from figjam-tokens directly), style.shape "ellipse".
True-outline type: connection anchors project onto the actual ellipse curve via
, not the bounding box. Label renders centered.

field (soft-defaults to "up" when omitted or invalid). Default geometry

Added in W5 — a 5-point regular polygon (one point up). Default geometry
Added in W5 — an 8-point regular polygon. Default geometry
Added in W5 — a 5-point star polygon (10 alternating outer/inner vertices). Default geometry
, inner radius a fixed fraction of outer (approximate, pending a pixel-reference check). Text slot is small and centered.
Added in W5 — a 12-point cross/plus polygon (equal-width horizontal and vertical bars). Default geometry
. Renders no visible label text — the cross's usable text area is too small, so the label is accessibility-only (hidesVisibleText).
Added in W5 — FigJam's "fat chevron arrow", pointing left or right via the type-only
field (soft-defaults to "right"). Distinct from arrow-shape: the two silhouettes diverged enough in FigJam's own picker to justify separate types rather than a shared weight field. Default geometry
's notchWidthRatio (0.25). Text slot is centered, offset away from the notch.
Added in W5 — a manila-folder silhouette: a rectangle with a small tab notch on the top edge. Default geometry

Bbox-fallback outline (the tab notch doesn't perturb connection anchors). Text slot is centered in the main body, below the tab.

Bbox-fallback outline. Text slot is centered on the front-most document silhouette.
Added in W5 — a downward-pointing pentagon (FigJam's SHIELD shapeType). Default geometry

Added in W5 — a 4-point trapezoid (FigJam's "manual operation" symbol): wider bottom edge, narrower symmetric top edge. Default geometry

Added in W5 — a 6-point regular hexagon, flat-top orientation (FigJam's "preparation" symbol). Default geometry
Added in W5 — a rectangle with two additional straight rule lines drawn inside near the top and left edges (FigJam's "internal storage" symbol). Default geometry

Outline is a plain rect — the interior rule lines are decoration only and don't perturb where a connector crosses the border. Text slot is centered in the remaining lower-right cell, below the top rule and right of the left rule.
Added in W5 — a circle with an inscribed "+" (FigJam's OR flowchart junction). Default geometry
Ellipse-silhouette true-outline type (shares the "junction" outline tier with summing-junction, both via
). The inscribed "+" is drawn from cardinal lines at 50%/50%, distinguishing it from summing-junction's "x". Renders no visible label text (accessibility-only).
Added in W5 — a circle with an inscribed "x" (FigJam's summing-junction flowchart symbol). Default geometry

Ellipse-silhouette true-outline type, same tier as or-junction. The inscribed "x" is drawn from diagonal lines at roughly 14.64%/85.36% (cos 45° scaled), distinguishing it from or-junction's "+". Renders no visible label text (accessibility-only).
Added in W5 — a horizontal-orientation cylinder (FigJam's ENG_QUEUE shapeType), distinct from database's vertical cylinder. Default geometry
Bbox-fallback outline (like database). Text slot is centered on the silhouette.

Added in W5 — the dog-eared/folded-corner page silhouette document used before W5 (FigJam's ENG_FILE shapeType), now split into its own type since document moved to a wavy-bottom silhouette instead. Default geometry

Rendered with the same CSS clip-path dog-ear notch document used to carry: polygon(0 0, 76% 0, 100% 24%, 100% 100%, 0 100%), asymmetric border radius (2px 8px 8px 8px). Bbox-fallback outline. Text slot is centered on the silhouette.
Added in W5 — a single icon-glyph family covering FigJam's 26-item Advanced picker tier, parameterized by a required
field (missing or unknown glyph is a hard validation error, unlike the soft-defaulted direction field). Default geometry
, the same label-below-glyph layout chip-icon/person/chat use, drawing from the

registry (26 glyphs: activity, archive, key, chat, cloud, cpu, database, display, mail, file, code, bolt, pin, phone, package, coin, shield, send, server, cube, gear, drive, terminal, person, wallet, globe). Bbox-fallback outline.
